narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the admissions model predicted a 68% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your law school application. Your profile shows strong undergraduate performance with a GPA of 3.4, which is above the typical student's 3.21, along with middle-class family income, full-time study plans, and your background as a white female applicant. However, several key factors stood out as concerns, with your LSAT score of 29.5—well below the average of 36.7—having the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far lower than the typical 5.53, which also significantly contributed to the failure outlook. Your Year 1 decile of 1, compared to the average 5.71, ranked third in impact and further reinforced the model's concerns about early academic struggles. Family income in the middle bracket followed closely, adding to the risk signal in a way that typical applicants' profiles didn't. Even your above-average undergraduate GPA, while positive in value, ended up nudging the prediction slightly toward failure in this context, ranking fifth in influence. Your racial background as white, which is common among applicants at about 84%, and gender as female, seen in 44% of cases, had smaller but still contributing effects toward the failure prediction, as did your full-time status, which is the norm at 93%. Overall, these factors combined to outweigh your strengths, unlike many successful applicants who show higher deciles, LSATs, and balanced profiles. To improve your bar exam chances moving forward, focus on intensive test preparation tailored to LSAT weaknesses, as boosting those skills could signal stronger analytical readiness for law school demands. Building consistent study habits now, perhaps through targeted academic coaching to mimic higher decile performance, would also help demonstrate growth. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score or gaining relevant experience could strengthen future applications, giving you a clearer path to success. Keep pushing—you have a solid GPA foundation to build on.
